question: What are current approaches to evaluating tool use in LLM agents?

claim_lines: - Measure whether the agent finishes the user task after using tools, often on realistic multi-step scenarios. This is the broadest metric and is still common in recent surveys. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s41019-025-00296-9?utm_source=openai))

- Benchmarks test whether the model chooses the right tool, produces a valid call, and fills arguments correctly. BFCL is a representative example focused on function-calling behavior, including multi-turn tool invocation. ([www2.eecs.berkeley.edu](https://www2.eecs.berkeley.edu/Pubs/TechRpts/2025/31680.html?utm_source=openai))

- Newer benchmarks evaluate agents over conversations and intermediate states, not just one-shot calls. ToolSandbox is designed for interactive, stateful tool use; ACEBench similarly splits evaluation into categories that cover normal, special, and agentic tool-use settings. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.04682?utm_source=openai))

- Beyond final success, researchers increasingly score tool-use trajectories: whether the agent asked for clarification when needed, avoided unnecessary tool calls, handled errors, and followed tool-specific constraints. Surveys now explicitly separate task completion, output quality, tool selection/invocation, and robustness/error handling. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s41019-025-00296-9?utm_source=openai))

- Some evaluations stress-test agents with misleading tools, malformed outputs, adversarial prompts, or high-stakes settings to see whether tool use is safe and reliable under failure conditions. ([link.springer.com](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s41019-025-00296-9?utm_source=openai))

In practice, the field is moving from **single-call, static benchmarks** toward **interactive, stateful, multi-step benchmarks with richer scoring**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.04682?utm_source=openai))
